coredata.dc:title: Enhanced removal and detection of benzo[a]pyrene in environmental water samples using carbon dots-modified magnetic nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Dezhi
Tammina, Sai Kumar
Li, Xiao
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: Magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) have already proven their efficacy in the disposal of a wide array of environmental contaminants in recent years. However, the difficulties in dispersibility and agglomeration of MNPs arising from its own physical and chemical properties limit its large-scale application. Herein, we fabricated the carbon dots/fatty acid-coated MNPs (CDs/C11-Fe3O4) through a facile and simple method. To utilize the advantage of carbon dots, these limitations can be mitigated by diminishing the size of MNPs and modifying the surface of MNPs. Detailed characterization including VSM, FT-IR, XPS and TEM conformed that the higher adsorption capacity of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 is mainly attributed to low average size (<8 nm), which is obviously lower than that of C11-Fe3O4 (about 13 nm). The CDs/C11-Fe3O4 showed higher adsorption performance than that of C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites (76.23 ng mg−1 for CDs/C11-Fe3O4 and 59.89 ng mg−1 for C11-Fe3O4). The adsorption processes of BaP on both C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites are exothermic, and well simulated by pseudo-second-order model. Moreover, the CDs/C11-Fe3O4 were also applied for the detection of BaP in large-volume water samples, which satisfies the China environmental protection standard, are promising candidates for water remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adsorption
Benzo[a]pyrene
Carbon dots
Hydrophilicity
Magnetic nanocomposites

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of BaP in 800 mL environmental water samples
Effect of the mixing time and sample volume

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Kunming University of Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was greatly supported by the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology ( 2016M20152118089 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic procedure of CDs-modified magnetic nanocomposites (CDs/C11-Fe3O4).
Phase separation of BaP adsorption by C11-Fe3O4 (a) and CDs/C11-Fe3O4(b) nanocomposites.
TEM image and size distribution of C11-Fe3O4 (A) and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 (B); (C) FT-IR analysis of CDs, C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites; (D) XPS analysis of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites.

The adsorption kinetics of BaP on C11-Fe3O4 (A) and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites (B), inset: fitting of pseudo-second-order rate kinetics model of adsorption of BaP by the prepared nanocomposites. Adsorption isotherm of BaP by C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites (C); adsorption isotherm following Langmuir model (D).
The typical chromatograms of the spiked waste water samples: (A) using MSPE and (B) using LLE.
Adsorption and determination of BaP in real samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Enhanced removal and detection of benzo[a]pyrene in environmental water samples using carbon dots-modified magnetic nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Environmental Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Environmental Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) have already proven their efficacy in the disposal of a wide array of environmental contaminants in recent years. However, the difficulties in dispersibility and agglomeration of MNPs arising from its own physical and chemical properties limit its large-scale application. Herein, we fabricated the carbon dots/fatty acid-coated MNPs (CDs/C11-Fe3O4) through a facile and simple method. To utilize the advantage of carbon dots, these limitations can be mitigated by diminishing the size of MNPs and modifying the surface of MNPs. Detailed characterization including VSM, FT-IR, XPS and TEM conformed that the higher adsorption capacity of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 is mainly attributed to low average size (<8 nm), which is obviously lower than that of C11-Fe3O4 (about 13 nm). The CDs/C11-Fe3O4 showed higher adsorption performance than that of C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites (76.23 ng mg−1 for CDs/C11-Fe3O4 and 59.89 ng mg−1 for C11-Fe3O4). The adsorption processes of BaP on both C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites are exothermic, and well simulated by pseudo-second-order model. Moreover, the CDs/C11-Fe3O4 were also applied for the detection of BaP in large-volume water samples, which satisfies the China environmental protection standard, are promising candidates for water remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: Benzo[a]pyrene (BaP) is one member of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), which is predominantly arises from diverse human activities, for instance urban runoff, industrial effluents and oil spillage (Carr et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2004). According to the National Primary Drinking Water Regulations (NPDWR, American), the maximum permissible limit of BaP in drinking water is 0.2 μg L−1 (U.S.E.P. Agency, 2009). The existence of this pollutant beyond the permitted levels in water leads to the mutagenesis and carcinogenicity, and now significant attention has been bestowed to explore the robust technologies to detect and remove BaP (Marrone et al., 2016; Teixeira et al., 2007). Various technologies, such as chemical (advanced oxidation processes) (Wols and Hofman-Caris, 2012), physical (adsorption and membrane filtration) (Liao et al., 2015; Sanches et al., 2011), and biological (Marrot et al., 2004; Verdin et al., 2004) treatments have been emerged for the BaP removal from wastewater. Among these, application of functional materials to adsorb the BaP has received more attention because of its operational simplicity, cost-effectiveness and high efficiency.

Nanomaterials used as adsorbents are a virtuous option for the adsorption and determination of contaminants owing to their unique physical and chemical properties (Khajeh et al., 2013). Over the last few decades, magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) are significant class of nanomaterials which are gathering astounding attention by virtue of its low operating cost, rapid separation, and convenient recycling. Many functionalized MNPs have been successfully applied to detect and remove metal ions as well as organic compounds (Wang et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2018a; Zhu et al., 2018). For instance, MNPs coated with hydrophobic materials are extensively used for adsorption and determination (Liao et al., 2015; Rozi et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2018b).

Our group had synthesized the fatty acid-coated MNPs to remove and detect the BaP in water samples (Liao et al., 2015). Conversely, there are certain inevitable limitations for the practical application of hydrophobic MNPs, especially when applied to detect and remove the trace level contaminations in large-volume water samples. The possible residue of the MNPs occurred in the adsorption process makes it very difficult to analyze the contaminants accurately. In large-volume water samples, the aggregation and dispersibility of MNPs also severely limits its applications. To overcome these challenges, further investigation is required to enhance the adsorption performance of MNPs in large-volume water samples.

Carbon dots (CDs) may be a finest improver to overcome these limitations in the process of adsorption by MNPs because of its own unique and significant properties (Dong et al., 2012a; Ju and Chen, 2014; Lim et al., 2015; Qu et al., 2012). CDs have been widely used in bioimaging, photocatalysis, sensors, optoelectronics and etc. (Fernando et al., 2015; Guo et al., 2016; Shen et al., 2012; Wang and Hu, 2014; Zhou et al., 2017). Furthermore, it is principally suitable to achieve the surface functionalization because of the existence of oxygen-containing groups (-COOH, -OH, etc) on the CDs surface (Dong et al., 2012b; Mashkani et al., 2018). Cai et al. indicated the silanized carbon dots-grafted silica (Sil-CDs) as stationary phase in HPLC to enhance the separation capability of chromatographic columns for the polar and hydrophilic samples (Cai et al., 2017). Mashkani et al. utilized the citric acid as a carbon source to prepare the magnetically N-doped carbon quantum dots for the extraction of lead ions (Mashkani et al., 2018). Carbon dots with multi-functional groups had been reported to apply them in proton exchange membranes (Jia et al., 2018). Along with these substantial applications, CDs also implied to diminish the size of the nanoparticles through knocking out some of the Fe3O4 nanocrystals on the surface (Bhattacharya and Deb, 2015). In general, when citric acid is used as the carbon source, the nanoparticles will possess an average size of less than 10 nm (Bourlinos et al., 2008a, 2008b). Thus, CDs prepared from the citric acid (CA) has promising potential to synthesize magnetic nanocomposites with smaller particle sizes (Dong et al., 2012b). Up to now, only a limited number of literatures about enhancing the extraction performance of MNPs with CDs have been reported (Rezaei et al., 2014; Mashkani et al., 2018; Mohapatra et al., 2015). The poor adsorption performance of hydrophobic magnetic nanocomposites in large-volume environmental water samples inspires us to design a novel CDs-functionalized MNPs.

Herein, we synthesized the CDs/C11-Fe3O4 as a novel reusable adsorbent for the reliable efficient adsorption and rapid determination of BaP. The scientific microwave was selected for the synthesis of CDs, because of its short time and high product yields (Huang et al., 2014; Zhai et al., 2012). Detailed characterization including VSM, FT-IR, XPS and TEM was used to reveal the difference of C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4. The effect of various parameters on BaP adsorption to C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanomaterials were investigated by batch experiments. Particularly, adsorption performance in large-volume water samples was also investigated to evaluate C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanomaterials. The reliability and feasibility of this method for BaP determination and removal in real water samples were further investigated.

Standard of BaP was supplied by Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Japan). All other chemicals used in the experiment were of analytical grade. Undecanoic acid (C11), iron (Ⅲ) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) and ammonium iron (Ⅱ) sulfate hexahydrate ((NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Ammonia solution (NH3·H2O) (25% w/w), acetone, acetonitrile (ACN), tetrahydrofuran (THF) and ethanol were all purchased from Guangzhou Jinhuada Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). Citric acid (CA) and urea were all purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). HPLC-grade acetonitrile and methanol were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany).

The magnetic hysteresis loop of the magnetic nanocomposites was obtained using vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, Lakeshore 3700, Lake Shore Cryotronics, Inc. USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (4000–400 cm−1) were recorded on a TENSOR27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data of products were collected on D/Max 2200 with Cu Kα radiation (γ = 1.5405 A) ranging from 2θ = 5–90° (Rigaku, America). The morphologies and microstructures of the sorbents were characterized by a Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI) electron microscope (200 kV, point-to point resolution 0.24 nm) (FEI, Holland). The BET surface area measurement were analyzed by a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 surface area (Quantachrome, USA). A Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. U.S.A.) was applied to investigate the composition and chemical state of the elements. Microwave system for pressure digestion was used to synthesize the CDs (TOPwave, analytikjena, Germany). Zeta potentials of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 in water were measured on a zeta potential analyzer ( Nano-ZS90, Malvern instruments, UK). Vacuum drying oven BPZ-6033 (Shanghai, China), air pump (AP-1688, Jeneca, China), vortexer (IKA Lab Dancer, China) and digital pH meter (Gttingen, Germany) were also used in the experiment.

Determination of BaP was carried out with Agilent 1260 series HPLC system (Agilent technologies, America). The HPLC separation was performed on a reversed-phase system (Agilent TC-C18 column, 150 mm × 4.6 mm i.d., 5 µm) at column temperature of 25 °C. The mobile phase was acetonitrile-water (75:25, v/v). The flow rate was set at 1 mL min−1, and the injection volume was 10 μL and the excitation/emission wavelengths of FLD were set at 290/430 nm.

CDs were synthesized through microwave heating which is described as follows: In brief, 1.5 g CA and 0.5 g urea were dissolved into 25 mL water, then the clear solution was transferred into a 60 mL Teflon microwave vessel. The sealed vessel was placed in a microwave digestion instrument, and the temperature of reaction was risen to 150 °C in 2 min and this condition was maintained for 50 min. The vessel was taken out from microwave digestion instrument after coming down to room temperature and then it was observed that the colorless solution turned pale yellow. Finally, the yellow colored solution was centrifuged at 12,000g for 10 min and filtered with a 0.22 µm membrane. The concentration of water-soluble CDs was set to 0.05 g mL−1 and then the obtained CDs were stored at 4 °C for further use.

A one-pot synthesis method was used for the preparation of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 ( Fig. 1). Initially, 1.69 g (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2·6H2O and 1.41 g FeCl3·6H2O were dispersed in 50 mL deionized water with a certain amount of CDs solution added. The suspension was heated at 80 °C under N2 atmosphere with vigorous stirring. Then, 100 mg of undecanoic acid (dissolved in 5 mL of acetone) and 4 mL of NH3·H2O was added gradually to the solution, respectively. The mixture was kept for 30 min at 80 °C. After it had cooled down to room temperature, the obtained precipitate was magnetically separated and washed with deionized water followed by ethanol. The precipitate was dried at 60 °C for 12 h to turn it into powder. Finally, the acquired powder/sorbent was redispersed in water and the concentration of materials was set to 30 mg mL−1. Similarly, 2 mL (0.10 g), 5 mL (0.25 g), 8 mL (0.4 g) and 10 mL (0.5 g) of the CDs was used to prepare the adsorbent by the same route, respectively. And the prepared adsorbent were defined as 0.1CDs/C11-Fe3O4, 0.25CDs/C11-Fe3O4, 0.4CDs/C11-Fe3O4 and 0.5CDs/C11-Fe3O4, respectively. To further understand the role of CDs on the adsorption experiment, C11-Fe3O4 was prepared without CDs by the same procedure specified as above.

Batch adsorption experiments were carried out by adding certain amount of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 (30 mg mL−1) into a 50 mL of BaP solution at various concentrations (in the range of 0.5–14 ng mL−1). The pH of the BaP solution was adjusted using 0.1 mol L−1 HCl and NaOH. The mixture were shaken for 20 s and then placed for a while to ensure the adsorption equilibrium attained. After reaching the adsorption-desorption equilibrium, nanocomposite was re-collected from the solution using an Nd-Fe-B strong magnet (50 × 25 ×5 mm) and the residual BaP concentration was measured by HPLC-FLD. Similarly, the effect of temperature, pH and ionic strength was studied at different temperature (15–45 °C), pH value (4–10) and NaCl concentrations (0–1.0 mol L−1). The effect of sample volume on the adsorption of BaP on C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 was also investigated using different volumes of water samples (50–1000 mL). The equilibrium adsorption isotherms of C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 were performed by varying the initial BaP concentrations at optimum pH. For kinetic studies, the adsorption experiments were also performed under 4 ng mL−1 BaP with 9 mg nanocomposite. The adsorption efficiency (η) and the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent were calculated according to the following equations:
(1)
η =
C
0
−
C
e
C
0
× 100 %
(2)
q
t
=
V (
C
0
−
C
t
)

(3)
q

=
V (
C
0
−
C
e
)
m
where q t and q e are the amount of BaP adsorbed on the nanocomposites at time “t” and equilibrium (ng mg−1), V is the volume of sample solution (mL), m is the weight of adsorbent (mg), and C 0, C t and C e are the initial, time “t” and equilibrium concentration of BaP in sample solution (ng mL−1), respectively (Yang et al., 2016).

For regeneration, the sorbents were magnetically collected after adsorption and then washed with deionized water for several times. The desorption of BaP was investigated by using different kinds of eluents including ACN and ACN-THF (v/v, 9:1, 8:2 and 7:3). To guarantee a good desorption efficiency, the mixture of eluent and sorbents were sonicated for 3 min. After magnetic separation, the concentration of BaP in eluent was also measured by HPLC-FLD. The regenerated adsorbent was subsequently used for next cycle of BaP removal. All the experiments were repeated five times under the similar conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of BaP in 800 mL environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of BaP in 800 mL environmental water samples were carried out through the auxiliary of a small air pump (1.6 L min−1). 1.5 mL of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 (45 mg) was added into water samples and then aerated for several minutes and placed for 5 min to ensure the adsorption equilibrium. After treatment, an Nd-Fe-B strong magnet (100 × 50 ×10 mm) was used to separate the adsorbent, and then adding a certain volume of eluent and sonicating for 3 min to ensure a good desorption. The concentrations of BaP in elution was measured by HPLC-FLD after filtered with 0.45 µm polyether sulfone filters.

To empower the practical application of CDs/C11-Fe3O4, the adsorbents should possess superparamagnetic properties. The magnetization curves are shown in Fig. S1A, and the saturation magnetizations of Fe3O4, C11-Fe3O4 and 0.25CDs/C11-Fe3O4 are 68.50, 48.94, and 46.82 emu g−1, respectively. With the addition of CDs, there is a slight difference in saturation magnetization between C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4. Compared with Fe3O4 NPs, the CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites still has a sufficiently strong magnetic response to the magnetic field. The contrast of the C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites dispersion phenomenon in the same experimental conditions is displayed in Fig. 2. For C11-Fe3O4 NPs, a hydrophobic layer could be observed within 1.5 min. After being attracted by a magnet, a thin hydrophobic layer formed on the surface of solution and did not disappear even after standing for 10 min. In case of CDs/C11-Fe3O4, the nanocomposite was well dispersed in water, and it was also re-collected completely from the solution, which demonstrated that the presence of CDs is helpful for the dispersion and re-collection of nanocomposites. Hence, the nanocomposite residue can be recovered completely without leaving any traces of material from the nanocomposites.

Fig. 3A and B show the TEM images of C11-Fe3O4 and 0.25CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites. As shown in Fig. 3A, C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposite has a broad size distribution. The diameters of C11-Fe3O4 particles sizes (inset of Fig. 3A) are in the range of 8–16 nm. For 0.25CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites, the TEM images (Fig. 3B) showed that the uniformed nanocomposites are well separated from each other and the average diameter is also less than 8 nm. There is a distinct contrasts between the morphology of C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites. This result led us to believe that the CDs leads to core sizes less than 10 nm thus diminishing the size of the nanocomposites, which is consistent with other literatures (Bhattacharya and Deb, 2015; Bourlinos et al., 2008a, 2008b; Mashkani et al., 2018). In addition, the measured BET surface areas are 88.2, 163.5, and 289.8 m2 g−1 for Fe3O4, C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites, respectively. One can conclude that the increased BET surface area of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 was attributed to the presence of CDs. Therefore, CDs/C11-Fe3O4 can provide large active areas for the BaP adsorption. The crystalline structure of CDs (inset of Fig. 3B), with lattice spacing of 0.32 nm, corresponded well to the (002) lattice planes of graphite, which also indicated that the CDs was successfully introduced to the surface of the magnetic nanomaterials (Zhang et al., 2017).

FT-IR analysis (Fig. 3C) was performed to characterize the CDs, C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4. For the FT-IR spectra of CDs, the peaks at 1170, 1403, 1549, 1646 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibrations of C-O, O-H, N-H and C˭O, respectively. The broad peak between 3000 and 3500 cm−1 indicates the presence of O-H and N-H. For C11-Fe3O4, the adsorption peaks of -CH2- and –CH3- are observed in the range 2800–3000 cm−1, and the band at 582 cm−1 is attributed to the Fe-O bonds. After the introduction of CDs, the characteristic peaks of C-N and N-H are also observed, testified to the existence of CDs in the prepared nanocomposites.

The surface elemental compositions and chemical states were studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 3D, the signals at 284.0 eV, 399.5 eV, 531.4 eV and 710.0 eV indicated the presence of C, N, O and Fe, respectively. In addition, four peaks at 283.4, 284.7, 286.5, and 287.4 eV in the high resolution spectrum of C1s could been assigned to C-C/C˭C, C-N, C-O and C˭O, respectively. These results designate the presence of hydrophilic groups, which agree well with the results of FT-IR.

Fig. S1-B depicts the XRD patterns of the prepared CDs, C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4. The diffraction patterns observed at 2θ of 30.2°, 35.5°, 43.1°, 53.9°, 57.3° and 62.9° are in agreement with the standard XRD data of Fe3O4 (Yang et al., 2017). The diffraction peaks of C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 could be indexed to the magnetite phase of iron oxide (JCPDS PDF no. 89–0951). The corresponding XRD pattern of the CDs displays a broad peak at around 2θ = 23.5°, which is corresponded to that of graphite carbon. For CDs/C11-Fe3O4, no diffraction peaks of the CDs are observed, which might be due to the amorphous nature of the carbon (Zhu et al., 2011).

Several parameters, such as the dosage of adsorbent, solution pH, ionic strength and temperature were studied to evaluate the adsorption efficiency of C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites. Experiments were conducted with the spiked water samples (50 mL) containing the BaP with the concentration of 2 ng mL−1. Fig. S2A shows the effect of the adsorbent dosage used in experiments. When the dosage of sorbents increased from 1.5 to 9 mg, the BaP adsorption efficiency was rapidly increased from 8.4% to 79.7% for C11-Fe3O4, and from 14.7% to 93.9% for CDs/C11-Fe3O4. When the adsorbent dosage increased at > 9 mg, adsorption percentage of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites had a plateau trend. Furthermore, CDs/C11-Fe3O4 showed higher adsorption capacity for BaP removal than C11-Fe3O4, which may be because of its higher surface area and more surface binding sites. It is well known that the adsorption capacity of adsorbent is usually proportional to its surface area (Jain et al., 2015; Singh et al., 2017). Therefore, 9 mg of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites was selected for the most suitable values.

The effect of solution pH was investigated in the range of 4–10. As shown in Fig. S2B, there was no significant change in the adsorption efficiency within pH 4–10. Fig. S2C shows zeta potential of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites at different pH values. The zeta potential of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites are negative. The adsorption efficiency for BaP does not change with increasing pH. It can be concluded that the BaP has stable chemical property, and the electrostatic interaction is not the main force for adsorption (Qiao et al., 2018). Therefore, the pH values of samples are not required to adjust. In addition, the effect of ionic strength was also studied by adding the different amounts of NaCl (0–1.0 mol L−1). The result (data not gave) indicated that the ionic strength could not affect the BaP removal efficiency. Both of our previous report and other literatures indicated that the adsorption efficiency was enhanced with the increase of the chain length of coating (Liao et al., 2015; Rozi et al., 2016; Qiao et al., 2018). These results are confirming that the hydrophobic interactions might be the driving force for adsorption of BaP.

The effect of temperature on the BaP adsorption was investigated from 15 to 45 °C. The observed results indicated (Fig. S3A) that the adsorption efficiency of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 declined with the increase of temperature, implying that the adsorption interaction of the adsorbent with BaP is an exothermic reaction. Since, adsorption efficiency of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 did not exhibit significant difference (P < 0.05) between 15 and 25 °C. Thereby, the adsorption experiments were carried out at 25 ± 2 °C.

The effect of other PAHs, such as benzo[a]anthracene (BaA) and indeno(1, 2, 3-cd)pyrene (IP) was also investigated. The results were listed in Table S1. It can been seen that no obvious change in removal efficiency was observed when the concentration of BaA and IP was 4 times of BaP. These results indicated that the adsorption and determination of BaP was not be influenced under the extremely low content of PAHs, which is consistent with previous research (Liao et al., 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the mixing time and sample volume
ce:section.ce:para: For large-volume water samples (800 mL), the dispersion of magnetic nanocomposite is one of the important parameters. To achieve this rapid dispersion of magnetic nanocomposites, the mixture was ventilated with the auxiliary of a small air pump. In this experiment, the mixing time means the time interval from the beginning of the ventilation and its end. The experiment was carried out at 25 °C with initial concentration of 0.1 ng mL−1, with a condition of pH = 6. It was observed (Fig. S3B) that around 93.4% of BaP was adsorbed after 12 min and one can obviously observe that the adsorption efficiencies of BaP on C11-Fe3O4 were lower than that of CDs/C11-Fe3O4, which might be due to the residue of C11-Fe3O4 (As shown in Fig. 2).

In the analysis of real samples, the sample volume is also one of the important parameters. To evaluate the adsorption performance of C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4, this paper examined the different volumes (50–1000 mL) of aqueous samples spiked with BaP (0.1 ng mL−1). All experiments were conducted under the optimum conditions by the same method. Fig. S4A displays the adsorption behavior of C11-Fe3O4, the BaP removal efficiency was decreased from 93.12% to 54.60% with increasing solution volume from 50 mL to 1000 mL. Compared to C11-Fe3O4, the appropriate amount of CDs markedly improved the adsorption efficiencies of the adsorbent at large-volume samples. This situation attributed to the hydrophilic nature of CDs, which enhanced the dispersibility of adsorbent and decreased the residue of nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. S4B, 0.5CDs/C11-Fe3O4 exhibited unsatisfactory adsorption performance, and about 51.43% (800 mL of solution) of BaP could be adsorbed, which indicated that the Van der Waals interactions between the sorbents and BaP may be affected (Liao et al., 2015). These results are explaining the role of CDs in the adsorption effect for enhancing the adsorption of C11-Fe3O4. An appropriate dosage of CDs results in enhanced removal efficiency. Therefore, 0.25CDs/C11-Fe3O4 was selected to use for the adsorption of BaP.

To investigate the rate of BaP adsorption on the C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4, experiments were conducted at an initial concentration of 4 ng mL−1, under the conditions of pH 6.0 and 25 °C. As shown in Fig. 4A, the adsorption capacity of prepared adsorbent raised rapidly in the first 2 min and then reached the adsorption equilibrium after 6 min. The further increase in contact time results no additional increase in adsorption efficiency. It was also noticed that the adsorption capacity of BaP on CDs/C11-Fe3O4 is higher than that of C11-Fe3O4, which might be the characteristic feature of smaller particle size and good dispersing capability of CDs/C11-Fe3O4.

To further explain the mechanism of BaP on C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4, two typical kinetic models, including pseudo-first-order model and pseudo-second-order model, were employed to simulate the kinetic data. The pseudo-first-order model and pseudo-second-order model can be expressed as the following equation:
(4)
ln (
q
e
−
q
t
) =
ln
q
e
−
k
1

(5)
t

t
=
1
k
2
q
e
2
+
t
q
e
where K 1 and K 2 are the constant of the pseudo-first-order model and pseudo-second-order model, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4A and B and Table S2, pseudo-second-order model is better at simulating the behavior of both C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 for BaP adsorption and possesses higher correlation coefficients (R > 0.9971) than pseudo-first-order model. This result indicated that the chemisorption process between the adsorbent and BaP is the rate limiting step.

The adsorptive capacities of C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 were also investigated to access their practicability as an adsorbent. The adsorption equilibrium was conducted by adding 9 mg of magnetic nanomaterial to 50 mL of water samples, with different concentrations of BaP solutions (0.5–14 ng mL−1). The adsorption curve of C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 with different concentrations of BaP solutions were presented in Fig. 4C. The adsorbed amount increased with initial concentration, and then reached the peak at BaP concentrations higher than 12 ng mL−1. As can be seen, the adsorption capacity of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 is higher than that of C11-Fe3O4, which was consistent with the aforementioned results. Two isotherm models, including (Langmuir and Freundlich) were applied to simulate the adsorption data. The experimental data can be well fitted by the Langmuir isotherm (Fig. 4D):
(6)
C
e
q
e
=
1
K
L
q
m
+
C
e
q
m
where q m is the maximum adsorption capacity (ng mg−1), K L, related to the adsorption free energy, is a Langmuir adsorption constant (mL ng−1). The Freundlich isotherm as the empirical equation, assumes the heterogeneous surface adsorption (Liu et al., 2018). The linear form of the Freundlich isotherm is expressed by the following equation:
(7)
ln
q
e
=
ln
K
F
+
1 n
ln
C
e
where K F (L mg−1) and n are Freundlich constants. K F represents the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent and n is a parameter related to the intensity of adsorption. Values of 0.1 < 1/n ≤ 0.5 indicates that it is easy to adsorb; values of 0.5 < 1/n ≤ 1 indicates that there is a difficulty with adsorption (Deng et al., 2013) and in the examined cases, the values of n < 0.5 (Table S3) showed that the CDs/C11-Fe3O4 had a good adsorption characteristic.

The isotherm constants for BaP adsorption on C11-Fe3O4 and CDs/C11-Fe3O4 are listed in Table S3. It can be seen from the fitting results that the Langmuir isotherm exhibited higher regression coefficient. The Langmuir isotherm model assumes that homogeneous adsorption occurs on the adsorbent surface which possess a finite number of identical sorption sites (Foo and Hameed, 2010). The reason may be that the surface of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 is uniform and homogeneous for BaP removal. On the basis of the Langmuir model, the maximum adsorption capacity of BaP on the CDs/C11-Fe3O4 is 76.23 ng mg−1, which is higher than that of C11-Fe3O4 (59.89 ng mg−1). The good adsorption capacity of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 highlights the potential application of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 as the adsorbent for BaP removal in large-volume environmental water samples.

To evaluate the recoverable potential of CDs/C11-Fe3O4, ACN and ACN-THF(v/v, 9:1, 8:2 and 7:3) were used as eluent. As shown in Fig. S5A, 92.2% of the adsorbed BaP was eluted by using 4 mL of ACN-THF (v/v, 8:2) in two steps (2+2 mL), while ACN and ACN-THF (7:3) desorbed 81.9% and 86.7% of BaP. The regeneration and reuse of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposite was continued up to 6th cycles (Fig. S5B). At the same time, the release of CDs and magnetic nanoparticles during adsorption-desorption cycles was measured. After 6 cycles, the collected CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposites were dried and weighed. The results showed that the mass loss is only about 4.1%. The XRD pattern, FTIR spectra and VSM analysis (Fig. S6) of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 after 6 cycles also did not find any obvious alteration, which confirmed that the CDs/C11-Fe3O4 had a good capacity of repeatability and regeneration. The easy magnetic separation and the excellent reusability and adsorption efficiency make CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposite as a potential candidate for cost-effective treatment of large-volume waste water.

Based on the adsorption and desorption of BaP on CDs/C11-Fe3O4 nanocomposite, the proposed method combined with HPLC-FLD analysis for the determination of BaP. The linear range of the method varied from 0.02 to 100 ng mL−1 with the determination coefficients of 0.9999. The limit of detection (LOD) (at signal-to-noise ratio of 3, S/N = 3) and limit of quantification (LOQ) (at signal-to-noise ratio of 10, S/N = 10) were 0.15 and 0.5 ng L−1, respectively. The precision of the method was investigated by measuring the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of intra-day and inter-day analysis. The repeatability (n = 6) was carried out by analyzing water samples the spiked with different concentrations of BaP (0.1 and 0.5 ng mL−1). The intra-day and inter-day RSDs varied from 3.3% to 5.3% and 3.1% to 4.6%, respectively. These results indicated that the proposed method has good accuracy for the analysis of BaP.

To demonstrate the practicability of CDs/C11-Fe3O4, the proposed method (MSPE) for BaP adsorption was evaluated in several water samples, including river water (water quality criteria III), lake water (water quality criteria IV), and waste water (water quality criteria V) (Environmental quality standards for surface water GB 3838–2002 China). Different amounts of BaP were spiked into the samples to evaluate the accuracy of the method. Furthermore, the BaP concentrations of the same samples were also analyzed simultaneously by liquid-liquid extraction (LLE) according to the China environmental protection standard (HJ 478–2009). The results are summarized in Table 1 and a t-test at the 95% confidence level revealed that the measured BaP concentrations in real water samples by the proposed method agreed well with the values determined by liquid-liquid extraction. The recoveries of BaP by using MSPE are in the range of 88.4–96.4%, and the RSDs is below 5.4%. These results confirmed that the present analytical method is reliable for the determination of BaP in large-volume water samples. Fig. 5 shows the typical chromatograms of the spiked waste water samples using MSPE and LLE, respectively. It is clearly observed that the proposed method can significantly eliminate the matrix effect, which designates that the proposed method can also used for the detection of trace level of BaP in water samples.

The removal efficiencies of this method (MSPE) were also compared with other methods reported in the literature (As shown in Table S4). It was not difficult to find that the removal efficiency is good. And the proposed method also has lower LODs than those of reported methods in literature. It reveals that CDs/C11-Fe3O4 is an efficient MSPE adsorbent compared to previously reported adsorbents.

In this work, a novel and reusable carbon dots based nanocomposite was synthesized through a one-pot synthesis method and used for the adsorption, determination and removal of Benz(a)Pyrene from the large-volume water samples. Microwave heating method was used for the synthesis of CDs, and the integration of CDs with magnetic nanocomposite leads to outstanding dispersion performance. The average diameter of the prepared nanocomposite is less than 8 nm. With the introduction of CDs, the possibility of carry-over occurred in the extraction process could be avoided. The saturation magnetization of CDs/C11-Fe3O4 is 46.82 emu g−1, showing effortless separation and tremendous reusability. The pH and ionic strength of aqueous solutions had no obvious influence on the BaP adsorption behavior. The appropriate ratio between CDs and magnetic nanocomposite played an important role in the adsorption of BaP for large-volume water samples. This method exhibited low LOD, good precision and acceptable adsorption capacity for the adsorption and determination of BaP. Overall, carbon dots decorated magnetic nanocomposites are the promising material for the efficient adsorption and determination of BaP in large-volume environmental water samples.
The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest. This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was greatly supported by the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (2016M20152118089).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ecoenv.2018.11.138.
